macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek HaSocher: Craftsmen vs. Workers and Initial Framework

micro_segments.title: Opening of new perek and terminology: umnin (craftsmen) vs. po'alim (workers)
Tosafot's distinction: po'alim as daily wage earners, umnin as broader category including kablan (job contractor)

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Deception Between Employer and Workers with No Financial Recourse

micro_segments.title: Basic rule: if employer and workers deceive one another, only complaint (taromet), no financial recourse
Example of davar avad (time-critical job): hiring donkey drivers or wagon drivers to transport items for wedding or funeral
Flax soaking scenario: workers must remove flax from water at precise time or it spoils
Tosafot: davar avad includes time-sensitive services even without tangible dollar loss (wedding chuppah, funeral)

macro_segments.title: Davar Avad Exception: Financial Consequences and Hiring Replacement Workers

micro_segments.title: When job is davar avad and workers back out: employer can hire replacements at exorbitant rates, workers must pay difference
Alternative remedy: deceive workers by promising higher wage to secure completion, then pay original amount

macro_segments.title: When Workers or Employer Back Out: The Concept of Yad Al HaTachtona (Lower Hand)

micro_segments.title: If workers back out mid-job: employer has lower hand, can withhold wages to cover hiring replacement workers
Rashi's explanation: employer can deduct replacement costs from workers' earned wages
If employer backs out: employer has lower hand, must pay workers proportional wages for work completed
Halacha: whoever backs out has lower hand (yado al hatachtona) – establishes principle of yad al tatachtona

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Deception Reconsidered as Workers Deceiving One Another Through Foreman

micro_segments.title: Gemara's initial interpretation: hitu zeh et zeh means workers deceived one another, not employer-employee deception
Scenario: employer hires foreman (socher), tells him go hire workers at lower rate; foreman tells workers higher rate and pockets difference
Analysis: if foreman says 'your pay depends on ba'al habayit', then foreman is not obligated to workers; workers' complaint is only against foreman

macro_segments.title: Foreman's Obligation and Worker's Complaint: Promise and Misrepresentation

micro_segments.title: Baraita: if foreman says 'your pay is on me', he becomes obligated; workers collect full amount from foreman
Foreman can then seek reimbursement from employer for benefit received, capped at going rate or what was authorized
If foreman misrepresents employer: foreman told them higher wage than authorized; workers trust foreman's word as representing employer

macro_segments.title: Workers' Complaint Threshold: Legitimate Grievance vs. Recourse

micro_segments.title: Scenario 1: foreman promised 4 but going rate is 3 – workers can legitimately claim they would have waited for 4-shekel job elsewhere
Scenario 2: foreman promised 4 but worker owns his own field – legitimate complaint because would not have left own work for standard rate
Scenario 3: foreman promised 4, they did extra-quality work – complaint valid but cannot assess quality after work done (furrow filled with water)
Common thread: all legitimate complaints require reason to expect more than standard rate, but employer only pays for actual benefit received

macro_segments.title: Whose Word Matters: Trust in Foreman vs. Principal's Intent (Gitin Analogy)

micro_segments.title: Question: when workers say 'we'll do like ba'al habayit says', do they trust foreman's words or trust ba'al habayit's actual intent?
Get analogy: messenger tells husband 'wife said I should be shaliach lekabbala' (receive) instead of leholacha (deliver); comparison to foreman case
Rav Ashi: Get case differs because messenger rejected his shlichus by changing the condition; not applicable to employment
Hypothetical: if messenger accepted more responsibility (leholacha instead of lekabbala), it would prove whether relying on messenger's word or principal's
Conclusion: no definitive answer from Get analogy to employment context; shlichus status of foreman remains unclear

macro_segments.title: Baraita: Specific Cases of Deception and Financial Consequences

micro_segments.title: Baraita states: workers or employer deceive one another; complaint but no financial recourse (agrees with Mishna opening)
Key qualifier: bemah devarim amurim – only true when workers have not yet arrived; deception is backing out before work begins
Once workers arrive: employer must pay full wages even if backing out or cannot provide work (נותן להם שכר משלם)

macro_segments.title: Work Already Begun: Proportional Payment and Dispute Over Worker Protection

micro_segments.title: If workers begin work then back out: pay them proportional to work completed (shamin lahem ma sheasu)
Example: contract to cut grain for 2 shekalim, cut half, left half – pay them 1 shekel for work done
Tanno Kamma position: workers always protected even mid-job; Tanna Kamma view represented in Mishna
Rabbi Dosa position: if workers back out mid-job and replacement costs more, employer withholds excess from workers' payment
Remaining work worth 1 shekel – worker gets 1 shekel; if 1.5 shekalim – still gets 1 shekel unless they finish for 2

macro_segments.title: Davar Avad (Time-Critical Work) and Remedy Limits

micro_segments.title: Even in davar avad, can hire replacements only if no other workers available; socher aleihen or mat'an remedies apply
Specific davar avad remedy: can promise workers double wage to keep them working, then pay original amount (deception justified)
Limit on hiring replacements: up to 40-50 zuz in extra costs; beyond that becomes unreasonable
If other workers readily available in market: even in davar avad, can only complain, not force workers to pay (ein aleihen ella taromet)

topical_tags.term: socher aleihen (hire replacements at worker's cost)
yored lesoch sdei chaveiro (improving neighbor's field without permission)
bemah devarim amurim (when is this ruling applicable)
shamin lahem ma sheasu (pay for work actually completed)
hamotzi mechavero alav haraya (burden of proof on claimant)
